walkthrough.title: Place the Game Board
walkthrough.text: Let's get Coimbra set up. First, place the large game board in the center of the table where everyone can reach it. This board has a lot going on, so take a moment to orient yourself. On the left side you will see four city locations stacked vertically: the castle at the top, followed by the upper city, the central city, and the lower city. On the right side is the pilgrim map with roads, stopovers, and monastery spaces. Along the top and bottom edges you will find the score track, influence tracks, and voyage card spaces.

walkthrough.title: Set Up the Monasteries
walkthrough.text: Sort the 24 monastery tiles by their backs into three stacks: I, II, and III. Shuffle each stack separately and place them face down beside the board. Draw 8 tiles from the I-stack and place one face up on each blue monastery space on the pilgrim map. Then draw 4 tiles from the II-stack and place one face up on each brown monastery space. Finally, draw 2 tiles from the III-stack and place one face up on each ruby monastery space. Return any remaining monastery tiles to the box.

walkthrough.title: Place the Influence Scoring Tiles
walkthrough.text: Shuffle the 4 influence scoring tiles and place one at random onto each influence scoring space above the 4 influence tracks on the game board. Make sure each tile's face-up side matches your player count: use the 2/3 side for two or three players, and the 4 side for four players. These tiles determine end-game scoring for influence track majorities.

walkthrough.title: Place the Die Tokens
walkthrough.text: Take the 5 die tokens. The placement depends on your player count. In a 4-player game, return all 5 die tokens to the box — they are not needed. In a 3-player game, place one token onto the upper city. In a 2-player game, place one token each onto the upper city and the central city, and one onto the lower city. These tokens act as neutral dice that remove character cards during Phase C.

walkthrough.title: Set Up the Voyage Cards
walkthrough.text: Shuffle the 15 voyage cards, draw 6 at random, and place one face up onto each voyage card space at the bottom of the game board. Return the remaining 9 voyage cards to the box. Each voyage card shows a task that scores victory points at end of game if you invest in it.

walkthrough.title: Prepare the Dice Pool
walkthrough.text: Take all 13 dice. In a 4-player game, place all 13 beside the board as the dice pool. In a 3-player game, remove 1 purple and 1 green die and place the remaining 11 beside the board. In a 2-player game, remove 1 gray, 1 orange, 2 purple, and 2 green dice and place the remaining 7 beside the board. The dice come in 5 colors: gray for guards, orange for coins, purple for pilgrim movement, green for victory points, and white which is wild.

walkthrough.title: Distribute Player Components
walkthrough.text: Each player chooses a color and receives: one player board placed in front of them, 3 die holders placed on the shutter spaces of the player board, 1 pilgrim set aside for now, 1 lion, 2 markers placed on space 7 of both the coin track and the guard track on the player board, and 25 disks. From the 25 disks, place one on space 0 of each of the 4 influence tracks on the game board and one on space 0 of the score track. The remaining 20 disks go beside your player board as your personal supply.

walkthrough.title: Determine Player Order
walkthrough.text: Determine the player order at random. The first player places their lion onto the player order flag marked 1, the second player onto flag 2, the third onto flag 3, and the fourth onto flag 4. This order matters immediately for the starting draft and for Phase B each round.

walkthrough.title: Prepare the Character Stacks
walkthrough.text: Sort the 56 character cards by their backs into three stacks: I, II, and III. Shuffle each stack separately. Take the II-stack and place it face down on top of the III-stack. This combined stack is the character stack, placed above the game board. Leave room beside it for a discard pile. Keep the I-stack separate for the starting draft in step 13.

walkthrough.title: Display the Favor Tiles
walkthrough.text: Display the 4 favor tiles in a row next to the upper left of the board beside the castle. Place the cap tile above the favor tile that shows 2 crowns. The favor tiles provide various immediate bonuses when taken from the castle during Phase C, and each tile also contributes 0 to 3 crowns toward player order in Phase D.

walkthrough.title: Set Up the Character Display
walkthrough.text: Draw 4 cards from the character stack and display them face up in a row to the left of the upper city. Draw another 4 cards and display them face up to the left of the central city. Draw a final 4 cards and display them to the left of the lower city. This creates a 3-row by 4-column grid of 12 character cards available for purchase during Phase C.

walkthrough.title: Distribute Crown Tokens
walkthrough.text: Take the 4 crown tokens, one for each character class: council member, merchant, cleric, and scholar. Distribute them to the character display by scanning cards from right to left, top to bottom — start with the fourth column from top to bottom, then the third column, then second, then first. Place each crown token on the first card you encounter of its matching class. If a class has no card in the display, that crown token is left unused this round.

walkthrough.title: Draft Starting Character Cards
walkthrough.text: Take the I-stack of start character cards. In a 4-player game, form 4 groups of 2 random cards. In a 3-player game, form 3 groups of 2 cards and return the remaining 2 to the box. In a 2-player game, form 2 groups of 2 cards and return the remaining 4 to the box. Display these groups face up beside the board.

walkthrough.title: Choose Starting Conditions
walkthrough.text: In reverse player order, each player chooses one of two actions: either take both character cards from one available group, or place their pilgrim onto a free starting corner in the center of the pilgrim map. After everyone has done one action, each player in normal player order carries out the remaining action. When done, every player should have 2 start character cards and their pilgrim on a different starting corner.

walkthrough.title: Resolve Start Character Bonuses
walkthrough.text: Finally, each player resolves their 2 start character cards. For each card, move your disk up on the matching influence track by the card's influence value. Then check the card's bonus. If it has an immediate bonus, gain it once and place the card in your immediate bonus zone to the right of your player board. If it has a Phase C bonus, place it below your player board in the Phase C bonus zone. You are now ready to play.

summary.title: Place the Game Board
bullets: Place the game board centrally
Orient: castle and cities on the left, pilgrim map on the right, score track along the edges

summary.title: Set Up the Monasteries
bullets: Sort 24 monastery tiles into stacks I, II, III — shuffle each separately
Place 8 from I-stack on blue spaces, 4 from II-stack on brown spaces, 2 from III-stack on ruby spaces
Return unused monastery tiles to the box

summary.title: Place the Influence Scoring Tiles
bullets: Shuffle 4 influence scoring tiles, place 1 above each influence track
Use 2/3 player side or 4-player side depending on player count

summary.title: Place the Die Tokens
bullets: 4 players: return all 5 die tokens to the box
3 players: place 1 token on the upper city
2 players: place tokens on upper city, central city, and lower city

summary.title: Set Up the Voyage Cards
bullets: Shuffle 15 voyage cards, draw 6, place 1 on each voyage space
Return remaining 9 to the box

summary.title: Prepare the Dice Pool
bullets: 4 players: all 13 dice
3 players: remove 1 purple + 1 green = 11 dice
2 players: remove 1 gray + 1 orange + 2 purple + 2 green = 7 dice
5 colors: gray (guards), orange (coins), purple (pilgrim), green (VP), white (wild)

summary.title: Distribute Player Components
bullets: Each player: 1 player board, 3 die holders on shutter spaces, 1 pilgrim, 1 lion
2 markers on space 7 of coin and guard tracks
Place 1 disk on space 0 of each of the 4 influence tracks and 1 on the score track
Keep remaining 20 disks as personal supply

summary.title: Determine Player Order
bullets: Randomly assign player order
Place lions on player order flags 1 through 4

summary.title: Prepare the Character Stacks
bullets: Sort 56 character cards by backs into I, II, III stacks — shuffle each
Stack II on top of III to form the character stack
Keep I-stack separate for starting draft

summary.title: Display the Favor Tiles
bullets: Place 4 favor tiles in a row beside the castle
Place cap tile above the favor tile with 2 crowns

summary.title: Set Up the Character Display
bullets: Draw 4 cards from character stack for each city row (upper, central, lower)
12 total character cards displayed in a 3x4 grid

summary.title: Distribute Crown Tokens
bullets: 4 crown tokens, one per class: council member, merchant, cleric, scholar
Scan cards right-to-left, top-to-bottom — place each crown on the first card of its class
If a class has no card in display, its crown token is unused

summary.title: Draft Starting Character Cards
bullets: Use I-stack: form groups of 2 cards (4 groups for 4p, 3 for 3p, 2 for 2p)
Display groups face up beside the board

summary.title: Choose Starting Conditions
bullets: Reverse player order: each player either takes a card group OR places pilgrim on a starting corner
Normal player order: each player does the remaining action
Everyone ends with 2 start character cards and pilgrim on a unique starting corner

summary.title: Resolve Start Character Bonuses
bullets: For each start card: advance on matching influence track by card's influence value
Immediate bonus cards go to the right of your player board
Phase C bonus cards go below your player board

walkthrough.title: Game Overview: Four Rounds, Six Phases
walkthrough.text: Coimbra is played over 4 rounds. Each round has 6 phases in order: A) Rolling Dice, B) Drafting and Placing Dice, C) Retrieving Dice, D) New Player Order, E) Influence Income, and F) Invest in a Voyage. After Phase F, if the character stack is empty the game ends. Otherwise, you refresh the character display and start a new round. The game always ends after exactly 4 rounds.

walkthrough.title: Phase A: Rolling Dice
walkthrough.text: The first player takes all dice from the dice pool and rolls them once. This creates the dice pool for the round. The values rolled are important — higher values give you priority placement but cost more when buying character cards, while lower values are cheaper but give you later picks.

walkthrough.title: Phase B: Drafting and Placing Dice
walkthrough.text: In player order, each player takes one die from the pool and places it in one of the 4 city locations: the castle, upper city, central city, or lower city. You place the die in one of your 3 die holders first, then onto the board. This continues in player order until each player has drafted and placed exactly 3 dice. The colors you choose determine your Phase E income — gray gives guards, orange gives coins, purple gives pilgrim movement, green gives victory points, and white is wild. The die value determines your placement order and your cost in Phase C.

walkthrough.title: Dice Placement Rules
walkthrough.text: The castle has a special placement rule: dice must form an ascending row from left to right, from low values to high values. So you place your die to the right of any equal or lower dice and to the left of any higher dice. The three city locations have the opposite rule: dice must form a descending row from left to right, high to low. Being farther left means you act first in Phase C. In the castle, a lower-value die placed early can land on the left. In the cities, a higher-value die lands on the left but costs more to buy a card.

walkthrough.title: Phase C: Retrieving Dice — Castle Action
walkthrough.text: Phase C evaluates the 4 city locations from top to bottom: castle first, then upper city, central city, and lower city. Within each location, dice are triggered left to right. The leftmost die's owner acts first. In the castle, your action is to take one of the available favor tiles and gain its immediate bonus. Taking a favor tile is free — no cost. Each favor tile also provides 0 to 3 crowns for Phase D. After taking a favor tile, retrieve your die and place it on an empty shutter space of your player board.

walkthrough.title: Phase C: Retrieving Dice — City Actions
walkthrough.text: In each of the three cities, your action is to acquire one character card from that city's row. The cost equals your die's value and must be paid in either guards or coins, depending on the icon in the card's upper left corner. After paying, gain the card's influence value on its matching influence track. Then resolve the card's bonus: immediate bonus cards go to your immediate bonus zone, Phase E bonus cards go below your board, and game end bonus cards go to the bottom right of your board. If a crown token is on the card, take it. Also check if the new card triggers a Phase C bonus from one of your start character cards.

walkthrough.title: The Distress Action
walkthrough.text: If you cannot or do not want to take a location's normal action in Phase C, you must instead take the distress action: gain up to 2 coins AND up to 2 guards by moving your markers forward on your player board. This is a safety net if you cannot afford any character cards, but it means you miss out on influence and card bonuses.

walkthrough.title: Die Tokens in Games with Fewer Than 4 Players
walkthrough.text: In 2- or 3-player games, die tokens placed in cities act as neutral dice. When a die token is the leftmost die in a city, instead of a player acting, the character card with the highest influence value in that row is removed to the discard pile. If there is a tie, the leftmost tied card is removed. Then the token is flipped face down and the next die is triggered. After all dice in a location are resolved, any remaining cards are discarded and any face-down tokens are flipped back up.

walkthrough.title: Phase D: New Player Order
walkthrough.text: Each player counts their total crowns from three sources: crown tokens collected this round, crowns on favor tiles taken this round, and crowns printed next to their current player order flag. The player with the most crowns moves their lion to flag 1, second most to flag 2, and so on. In case of a tie, the relative order among tied players is reversed. Crowns reset at the end of each round when new favor tiles and crown tokens are distributed.

walkthrough.title: Phase E: Influence Income
walkthrough.text: In the new player order, each player collects income from their 3 dice sitting on the shutter spaces. For each die, check its color: gray gives guards, orange gives coins, purple gives pilgrim movement steps, green gives victory points, and white lets you choose any one track's income. The amount you receive depends on how far up your disk is on the matching influence track — check the reward box next to your disk's current position. After collecting all 3 incomes, return the dice to the pool and leave the empty die holders on the shutter spaces.

walkthrough.title: Phase F: Invest in a Voyage
walkthrough.text: In player order, each player may invest in one voyage card. Choose any voyage card at the bottom of the board that does not already have your disk on it. Pay the cost shown in the card's upper left corner in either coins or guards as indicated by the banner. Place one of your disks on the card's rose to mark your investment. At game end, each invested voyage card scores victory points based on how well you fulfilled its task. You may only invest once per round, and you may invest in the same voyage card that other players have invested in.

walkthrough.title: End of Round and Refreshing the Display
walkthrough.text: After Phase F, check if the character stack is empty. If so, the game ends after 4 rounds. Otherwise, set up the next round by repeating the character display setup: display 4 new favor tiles beside the castle with the cap tile, draw 12 new character cards in a 3x4 grid for the three cities, and distribute the 4 crown tokens to the display as in setup steps 10, 11, and 12.

walkthrough.title: Moving Your Pilgrim
walkthrough.text: Whenever you receive pilgrim movement steps as a reward, move your pilgrim along the roads of the pilgrim map up to that many steps. One step moves from your current location to the next location along a road. There are two kinds of locations: stopovers where nothing happens, and monasteries where you must place a disk if you do not already have one there. Placing a disk gives you that monastery's bonus immediately. You may change direction at any time, move fewer steps than allowed, and pass through locations with other pilgrims. You cannot move backward along the one-way roads leaving Coimbra's center.

walkthrough.title: Resource Limits and Influence Caps
walkthrough.text: Your guard and coin tracks on your player board have a maximum of 20 each. You start at 7 on both tracks. You can never exceed 20 — any excess is forfeited. Your influence tracks on the game board have a maximum of 15 — any excess beyond space 15 is also forfeited. On each influence track, if two disks share a space, the one lower in the stack is considered ahead because it arrived first.

walkthrough.title: End of Game: Final Scoring
walkthrough.text: After the fourth round, score in four categories. First, score victory points for each voyage card you invested in based on its task. Second, score influence track majorities using the influence scoring tiles — first, second, and possibly third place each earn points. Third, score sets of diploma icons found on your character cards and monasteries: a set of 5 different diplomas scores 12, four scores 8, three scores 4, two scores 2, and singles score 1 each. Wild diplomas can substitute for any icon. Fourth, resolve game end bonus character cards. Finally, sum your remaining guards, coins, and crowns, divide by 2 (rounded down), and add that as victory points. The player with the most points wins. Ties go to the player earlier in player order.

summary.title: Game Overview: Four Rounds, Six Phases
bullets: 4 rounds, each with 6 phases: A) Roll, B) Draft, C) Retrieve, D) Order, E) Income, F) Voyage
Game ends after Round 4 when the character stack runs out

summary.title: Phase A: Rolling Dice
bullets: First player rolls all dice from the pool at once
Rolled values determine placement order and card costs

summary.title: Phase B: Drafting and Placing Dice
bullets: In player order, draft 1 die at a time into a die holder, place on a city location
Each player places exactly 3 dice per round
Dice colors: gray=guards, orange=coins, purple=pilgrim, green=VP, white=wild

summary.title: Dice Placement Rules
bullets: Castle: ascending left to right (low to high)
Upper/Central/Lower city: descending left to right (high to low)
Farther left = act first in Phase C, but higher value = higher cost in cities

summary.title: Phase C: Retrieving Dice — Castle Action
bullets: Castle evaluated first, dice triggered left to right
Take 1 available favor tile (free) and gain its immediate bonus
Favor tiles add 0-3 crowns for Phase D player order
Retrieve die to a shutter space on player board

summary.title: Phase C: Retrieving Dice — City Actions
bullets: Acquire 1 character card from the row matching your city location
Cost = your die's value, paid in guards or coins per card's icon
Gain influence value on the card's class track
Place card in correct zone: immediate, Phase E, or game end

summary.title: The Distress Action
bullets: If you cannot or do not want the normal action: gain up to 2 coins AND up to 2 guards
Safety net — but you miss influence and card bonuses

summary.title: Die Tokens in Games with Fewer Than 4 Players
bullets: Die tokens act as neutral dice in cities
When triggered: remove highest-influence-value card from that row to discard
Ties: remove leftmost tied card
Token flips face down, then next die triggers

summary.title: Phase D: New Player Order
bullets: Count crowns: crown tokens + favor tile crowns + player order flag crowns
Most crowns = flag 1, second = flag 2, etc.
Ties: relative order is reversed among tied players

summary.title: Phase E: Influence Income
bullets: Activate each of your 3 dice by color — gain income from matching influence track
Income amount = reward box next to your disk's current position on that track
White die: choose any one influence track's income
Return dice to the pool after collecting

summary.title: Phase F: Invest in a Voyage
bullets: Optionally invest in 1 voyage card per round (pay coins or guards as shown)
Place a disk on the card's rose to mark investment
Scores VP at game end based on the card's task

summary.title: End of Round and Refreshing the Display
bullets: If character stack is empty, game ends — proceed to final scoring
Otherwise: refresh favor tiles, deal 12 new character cards, redistribute crown tokens

summary.title: Moving Your Pilgrim
bullets: Move along roads up to the number of steps received
Stopovers: nothing happens — keep moving
Monasteries: place a disk (if you do not have one there) and gain its bonus
May change direction, move fewer steps, pass through other pilgrims

summary.title: Resource Limits and Influence Caps
bullets: Guards and coins: max 20 each on player board (start at 7)
Influence tracks: max 15 — excess forfeited
Stacking order on shared spaces determines tiebreaks

summary.title: End of Game: Final Scoring
bullets: 1) Voyage card tasks
2) Influence track majorities (1st/2nd/3rd place per scoring tile)
3) Diploma sets: 5=12, 4=8, 3=4, 2=2, 1=1 VP; wild diplomas count as any
4) Game end bonus character cards
5) (Guards + Coins + Crowns) / 2 rounded down = VP
Tiebreak: earlier in player order wins

walkthrough.title: Welcome to the Practice Round
walkthrough.text: In this practice tutorial, we will walk through a scripted first round of Coimbra with 3 players — Phil, Eve, and Chris. Three players is the recommended player count for learning. We will cover all 6 phases of a round so you can see how dice drafting, card acquisition, pilgrim movement, and income collection work in practice. Follow along even if you have a different number of players at your table.

walkthrough.title: Starting Resources
walkthrough.text: Each player starts with 7 coins and 7 guards on their player board tracks, 20 disks in their personal supply, a pilgrim on a starting corner, and 2 start character cards already resolved. Phil has his disks on space 2 of the council member track and space 1 of the cleric track from his start cards. Eve is at space 2 on the merchant track and space 1 on the scholar track. Chris is at space 1 on the council member track and space 2 on the scholar track. Everyone has their lion on a player order flag — Phil is first, Eve second, Chris third.

walkthrough.title: Phase A: Phil Rolls the Dice
walkthrough.text: Phil is the first player, so he takes all 11 dice from the pool and rolls them. The results are: gray 5, gray 4, gray 2, orange 5, orange 3, orange 1, purple 4, purple 2, green 3, green 1, and white 3. That is our dice pool for the round. Remember, each player will draft 3 dice from this pool.

walkthrough.title: Phase B: Phil's First Die — Gray 5 to the Upper City
walkthrough.text: Phil goes first. He scans the character display and sees a merchant card with influence value 3 in the upper city row that costs guards. He wants that card, and a high die value means he will be placed farther left, giving him priority. He picks the gray 5 from the pool, puts it in a die holder, and places it in the upper city. Since it is the first die there (aside from the die token for the 3-player game), it goes to the left of the token. The gray die means Phil will receive guard income in Phase E.

walkthrough.title: Phase B: Eve's First Die — Orange 5 to the Central City
walkthrough.text: Eve sees a cleric card in the central city with influence value 2 that costs coins. She takes the orange 5 and places it in the central city. That is an expensive pick — she will need to pay 5 coins to acquire the card — but she will act first in that location. The orange die gives her coin income in Phase E, which helps her recover.

walkthrough.title: Phase B: Chris's First Die — Green 3 to the Castle
walkthrough.text: Chris decides to place a die in the castle to grab a favor tile. He takes the green 3. In the castle, dice go in ascending order left to right, so the 3 goes into position. A green die will give Chris victory point income in Phase E. The castle action is free — no cost to take a favor tile.

walkthrough.title: Phase B: Remaining Dice Placement
walkthrough.text: The drafting continues in player order for two more rounds. Phil takes the gray 4 and places it in the castle, and the white 3 for the lower city. Eve takes the purple 4 for the upper city and the orange 3 for the lower city. Chris takes the purple 2 for the central city and the gray 2 for the lower city. Each player now has 3 dice on the board and no die holders left. Two dice remain in the pool — the orange 1 and green 1 — they are unused this round.

walkthrough.title: Phase C: Evaluating the Castle
walkthrough.text: Now we evaluate locations top to bottom, starting with the castle. Dice in the castle are in ascending order: Chris's green 3 is left, Phil's gray 4 is right. Chris goes first. He takes the favor tile that grants 4 victory points as an immediate bonus and has 2 crowns. He moves his score track disk forward 4 and retrieves his die to a shutter space. Then Phil acts — he takes the favor tile that reduces voyage investment costs by 2. He retrieves his die to a shutter space.

walkthrough.title: Phase C: Evaluating the Upper City
walkthrough.text: Next is the upper city. Dice are in descending order: Phil's gray 5 is leftmost, then the die token, then Eve's purple 4. Phil acts first. He wants the merchant card with influence value 3. Its cost icon shows guards, and his die is a 5, so he must pay 5 guards, moving his guard marker from 7 to 2. He gains 3 spaces on the merchant influence track. The card has an immediate bonus of 4 coins, so he moves his coin marker from 7 to 11. He places the card in his immediate bonus zone. The die token is triggered next, removing the card with the highest influence value from the row. Then Eve acts with her purple 4.

walkthrough.title: Phase C: Eve's Mistake — Overspending on Guards
walkthrough.text: Eve's purple 4 in the upper city means she must pay 4 of whatever resource the card requires. She spots a council member card that costs guards and has an influence value of 2. She pays 4 guards, moving her guard marker from 7 to 3. She gains the influence and places the card. But here is the problem — Eve now has only 3 guards left, and she still has a die in the lower city where she planned to buy another guard-cost card. She will likely not be able to afford it. She overspent without checking her remaining dice positions.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Plan All Three Dice Before Spending
walkthrough.text: The takeaway from Eve's mistake: before Phase C begins, look at all 3 of your dice and mentally budget your guards and coins. If two of your dice are in cities with guard-cost cards, you need enough guards to cover both values. Eve should have taken the cheaper card with her 4-value die and saved guards for the lower city. In Coimbra, planning your spending across all three dice is critical because there is no way to gain resources mid-Phase C.

walkthrough.title: Phase C: Evaluating the Central and Lower Cities
walkthrough.text: The central city has Eve's orange 5 and Chris's purple 2. Eve acts first, paying 5 coins for the cleric card she wanted. Her coin marker drops from 7 to 2. She gains 2 on the cleric influence track and places the card. Chris then pays 2 guards for a scholar card. In the lower city, Phil's white 3 goes first. He picks a card costing 3 coins. Eve's orange 3 is next — she takes a card costing 3 coins from her remaining 2 coins... but she cannot afford it. She must take the distress action: gain up to 2 coins and up to 2 guards instead.

walkthrough.title: Eve's Second Mistake — Forced into Distress
walkthrough.text: Eve was forced to take the distress action because she spent too many guards in the upper city and too many coins in the central city. The distress action only gives up to 2 coins and 2 guards — far less valuable than an actual character card with influence, bonuses, and potential diplomas. This is the consequence of not budgeting resources across all three dice placements.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: The Distress Action Is a Last Resort
walkthrough.text: The distress action exists as a safety net, but taking it means you lose out on influence track advancement, card bonuses, and diploma icons. If you find yourself being forced into distress, it usually means you made a resource planning error earlier. Always check your guards and coins against your die values before Phase C begins. A lower-value die in a city is cheaper and sometimes the smarter choice.

walkthrough.title: Phase D: Counting Crowns
walkthrough.text: Now we count crowns for the new player order. Phil has: 1 crown from his favor tile, 1 crown from a crown token he collected on a character card, and 1 crown from his current order flag — total of 3. Eve has: 0 crowns from favor tiles, 0 crown tokens, and 0 from her order flag — total of 0. Chris has: 2 crowns from his favor tile, 1 crown token, and 0 from his order flag — total of 3. Phil and Chris tie at 3, so their relative order reverses. Chris moves to flag 1, Phil to flag 2, Eve stays at flag 3.

walkthrough.title: Phase E: Collecting Influence Income
walkthrough.text: In the new player order, Chris goes first. His 3 dice are green, purple, and gray. For the green die, he checks his position on the green scholar influence track (space 2) and gains the income shown in the reward box next to space 2: a small amount of victory points. For the purple die, he checks the purple cleric track and gains pilgrim steps. For the gray die, he checks the gray council member track and gains guards. Phil collects income from his two gray dice and one white die. For the white die, he chooses the track with the best reward. Eve collects from her orange, purple, and orange dice.

walkthrough.title: Eve's Third Mistake — Ignoring Die Color Strategy
walkthrough.text: Notice that Eve took two orange dice and one purple die. Both orange dice give coin income based on her merchant influence track position. But Eve is only at space 2 on the merchant track — her income is low. If she had taken a gray die instead of the second orange, she could have diversified her income. Worse, she has no green dice at all, so she receives zero victory point income this round. Meanwhile Phil, with two gray dice and a high council member track position, gained a large guard income that will fund next round's purchases.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Match Die Colors to Your Strongest Tracks
walkthrough.text: The income you gain in Phase E is proportional to your position on the matching influence track. A green die is only valuable if you are high on the scholar track. An orange die is only valuable if you are high on the merchant track. When drafting dice in Phase B, look at your influence track positions and prioritize colors where you have advanced the most. The white die is especially powerful because it lets you choose whichever track has the highest reward.

walkthrough.title: Phase F: Investing in Voyages
walkthrough.text: Chris goes first in the new player order. He looks at the 6 voyage cards. He finds one that rewards 1 victory point per monastery with his disk above it. He has been planning a pilgrim-heavy strategy, so this fits. The card costs 6 coins — he spends 6, moving his coin marker down, and places a disk on the card. Phil has the favor tile that reduces voyage costs by 2, so he invests in a 7-guard-cost voyage for only 5 guards. Eve passes because she is low on both guards and coins after her overspending.

walkthrough.title: End of Round 1: Refreshing the Display
walkthrough.text: Round 1 is complete. The character stack still has cards, so we set up Round 2. Draw 12 new character cards from the character stack and display them in the 3x4 grid. Place new favor tiles beside the castle. Distribute crown tokens to the new display. Return unused crown tokens. Return all favor tiles from players. The dice are already back in the pool from Phase E. Chris is now first player for Round 2.

walkthrough.title: What We Learned from Round 1
walkthrough.text: Phil played a solid round — he spent wisely, acquired high-influence cards, used a favor tile to discount his voyage, and matched die colors to his strong tracks. Chris made smart choices with the castle and voyage investment. Eve made three common mistakes: overspending on guards without budgeting all three dice, getting forced into the distress action, and ignoring the relationship between die color and influence track position. All three of these mistakes compound — poor resource planning leads to distress, and poor die color choices mean less income to recover.

walkthrough.title: Congratulations
walkthrough.text: You have now seen a full round of Coimbra with all 6 phases in action. You have watched dice drafted and placed, character cards acquired, the pilgrim map explored, income collected, and a voyage invested in. You have also seen three common beginner mistakes and learned how to avoid them. You are ready to continue playing on your own.

walkthrough.title: Before You Begin Your Voyage
walkthrough.text:
Before you dive into a full game, here are three things that can help.

First — check out General Tips for strategy basics on die drafting, influence track priorities, and resource management.

Second — reference the Appendix for a quick guide to all dice colors, character classes, favor tile bonuses, and game terms.

And most importantly — ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Have fun!

summary.title: Welcome to the Practice Round
bullets: Scripted first round with 3 players: Phil, Eve, and Chris
3 players is the recommended count for learning
Covers all 6 phases of a complete round

summary.title: Starting Resources
bullets: Each player starts at 7 coins and 7 guards
20 disks in personal supply, pilgrim on a starting corner
2 start character cards already resolved, influence tracks advanced

summary.title: Phase A: Phil Rolls the Dice
bullets: First player rolls all 11 dice (3-player count)
Dice pool for the round is established in one roll

summary.title: Phase B: Phil's First Die — Gray 5 to the Upper City
bullets: High die = acts first in the city but costs more
Gray die = guard income in Phase E
Placed leftmost in descending city order

summary.title: Phase B: Eve's First Die — Orange 5 to the Central City
bullets: Expensive pick at 5 coins — but acts first in central city
Orange die = coin income in Phase E to recover

summary.title: Phase B: Chris's First Die — Green 3 to the Castle
bullets: Castle action is free — no cost to take a favor tile
Green die = victory point income in Phase E
Castle uses ascending order: low values on the left

summary.title: Phase B: Remaining Dice Placement
bullets: Each player places 2 more dice in player order
Total: 9 dice placed (3 per player), 2 unused from pool

summary.title: Phase C: Evaluating the Castle
bullets: Castle first, leftmost die acts first
Chris takes a 4 VP favor tile with 2 crowns
Phil takes the voyage cost reduction favor tile

summary.title: Phase C: Evaluating the Upper City
bullets: Phil pays 5 guards for a merchant card — gains 3 influence and 4 coins
Die token removes highest influence card from the row
Eve acts third with her purple 4

summary.title: Phase C: Eve's Mistake — Overspending on Guards
bullets: Eve pays 4 guards for a council member card, dropping to 3 guards
She still has another die in a city that may need guards
Didn't plan spending across all 3 dice positions

summary.title: Lesson: Plan All Three Dice Before Spending
bullets: Before Phase C, mentally budget guards and coins across all 3 dice
No way to gain resources mid-phase — plan ahead
Lower-value dice are cheaper; sometimes the smarter choice

summary.title: Phase C: Evaluating the Central and Lower Cities
bullets: Eve pays 5 coins in central city — coin marker drops to 2
Eve cannot afford a card in the lower city

summary.title: Eve's Second Mistake — Forced into Distress
bullets: Distress action: gain up to 2 coins and 2 guards — far less than a card
Lost influence advancement, card bonus, and diploma opportunity
Consequence of overspending in earlier locations

summary.title: Lesson: The Distress Action Is a Last Resort
bullets: Distress means no influence gain, no bonuses, no diplomas
If forced into distress, you made a resource error earlier
Always verify you can afford all 3 planned card purchases

summary.title: Phase D: Counting Crowns
bullets: Sum crowns from: tokens, favor tiles, and player order flag
Most crowns = first player next round
Ties: relative order reverses

summary.title: Phase E: Collecting Influence Income
bullets: Each die's color maps to an influence track
Income = reward box next to your disk's position on that track
White die: choose any track's income

summary.title: Eve's Third Mistake — Ignoring Die Color Strategy
bullets: Two orange dice but low on the merchant track = low coin income
No green dice = zero VP income this round
Didn't match die colors to her strongest influence tracks

summary.title: Lesson: Match Die Colors to Your Strongest Tracks
bullets: Income scales with influence track position — draft colors where you are highest
White die is powerful: pick whichever track has the best reward
Diversify colors to avoid wasting income potential

summary.title: Phase F: Investing in Voyages
bullets: Chris invests in a monastery-counting voyage for 6 coins
Phil uses his favor tile discount to invest for only 5 guards
Eve passes — too low on resources after overspending

summary.title: End of Round 1: Refreshing the Display
bullets: Draw 12 new character cards, new favor tiles, redistribute crown tokens
Dice already returned to pool from Phase E
Chris is first player for Round 2

summary.title: What We Learned from Round 1
bullets: Phil played well: budgeted resources, matched die colors, used favor tile for voyage discount
Eve's 3 mistakes: overspent guards, forced into distress, ignored die color strategy
All three mistakes compound — poor planning cascades through later phases

summary.title: Congratulations
bullets: You have seen all 6 phases of a complete round
Watched dice drafted, cards acquired, pilgrim moved, income collected, voyage invested
You are ready to continue playing on your own

summary.title: Before You Begin Your Voyage
bullets: Check out General Tips for strategy on die drafting and resource management
Reference the Appendix for dice colors, character classes, and game terms
Ask your personal GM Guide anytime — tap Q&A for instant answers during play

walkthrough.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
walkthrough.text: Before diving into strategy, here are the most common mistakes new Coimbra players make. First, not budgeting guards and coins across all three dice before Phase C — this leads to distress actions and wasted turns. Second, ignoring the relationship between die color and influence track position, resulting in poor Phase E income. Third, hoarding resources instead of investing in voyages. Fourth, neglecting the pilgrim map and missing out on monastery bonuses and diploma icons. Fifth, chasing high-influence cards without considering their actual bonus value. Keep these in mind to avoid the biggest beginner traps.

walkthrough.title: Die Drafting Strategy
walkthrough.text: When drafting dice in Phase B, consider three factors simultaneously. First, the die value: high values give you priority placement but cost more in the cities. Low values are cheaper but you act later and may lose the card you want. Second, the die color: it determines your Phase E income. Match colors to influence tracks where you are advanced. Third, placement location: the castle gives a free favor tile, while the cities let you acquire character cards. A common beginner error is drafting purely for value or color without considering all three factors together.

walkthrough.title: Resource Management
walkthrough.text: You start each game with 7 guards and 7 coins, and the maximum is 20 for each. Your primary way to spend these is paying for character cards in Phase C and investing in voyages in Phase F. Your primary way to earn them is Phase E income. The key insight is that your income depends on your influence track position, which depends on the character cards you acquire, which costs the resources you earn from income. This circular economy means you need to invest wisely early to build a sustainable resource flow. Never let both your guards and coins drop below 3 going into Phase C.

walkthrough.title: Influence Track Priorities
walkthrough.text: The four influence tracks give different Phase E income types: gray council members give guards, orange merchants give coins, purple clerics give pilgrim steps, and green scholars give victory points. In the early game, the gray and orange tracks are most important because guards and coins fuel card acquisition. The purple track becomes valuable once you have pilgrim-dependent voyages or monastery bonuses to chase. The green track is a late-game powerhouse — high positions on the scholar track generate massive VP income in Rounds 3 and 4. Focus on one or two tracks early and branch out later.

walkthrough.title: The Pilgrim Map
walkthrough.text: Your pilgrim is a secondary scoring engine that many beginners neglect. Monasteries provide powerful one-time bonuses including influence track advances, victory points, diploma icons, and special abilities. The key is to plan your pilgrim route early. Look at which monastery bonuses synergize with your character card strategy. Some monasteries give you 3 spaces up on an influence track for free — that is enormous. Others let you score voyage cards early or gain wild diplomas. Purple dice and certain character card bonuses provide pilgrim movement, so plan your die drafting accordingly.

walkthrough.title: Character Card Evaluation
walkthrough.text: Not all character cards are equal. When evaluating a card, consider five factors. First, its class — does it advance an influence track you care about? Second, its influence value — higher values push you up faster. Third, its bonus type — immediate bonuses give you a one-time boost, Phase E bonuses compound every round, and game end bonuses reward long-term strategy. Fourth, its cost type — does it require guards or coins, and can you afford the die value? Fifth, diploma icons — sets of 5 different diplomas score 12 points, which is massive. Phase E bonuses are generally the most valuable because they trigger every round.

walkthrough.title: Crown and Player Order Strategy
walkthrough.text: Player order is determined by crowns in Phase D. Going first means you draft dice first in Phase B and invest in voyages first in Phase F. Crown sources include crown tokens on character cards, favor tile crowns, and crowns next to your player order flag. Going first is especially important in Round 1 and Round 4 — early rounds because you get first pick of the dice, and late rounds because you secure the best remaining voyage investments. However, the castle is the main source of crowns, so balance castle visits with city card acquisitions.

walkthrough.title: Voyage Investment Timing
walkthrough.text: Voyage cards score based on end-game conditions: monasteries visited, diploma counts, influence track positions, character card counts, and more. The cost to invest ranges from 5 to 8 guards or coins. Invest early if you can afford it — there is no discount for waiting, and other players may invest in the same card, so it is not a race. But do invest. Passing on voyages every round means missing out on potentially 8 to 12 victory points per card. The favor tile that reduces voyage costs by 2 is extremely powerful — if you see it, consider placing a die in the castle to grab it.

summary.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
bullets: Don't enter Phase C without budgeting guards and coins across all 3 dice
Don't ignore die color vs. influence track position
Don't hoard resources — invest in voyages
Don't neglect the pilgrim map and monastery bonuses
Don't chase high-influence cards without checking bonus value

summary.title: Die Drafting Strategy
bullets: Three factors: die value (cost/priority), die color (Phase E income), placement location
High value = acts first in cities but costs more to buy
Match die color to your strongest influence tracks
Castle dice are free actions; city dice have costs

summary.title: Resource Management
bullets: Start at 7 guards, 7 coins — max 20 each
Spend: Phase C card costs + Phase F voyage investments
Earn: Phase E income scales with influence track position
Never let both guards AND coins drop below 3 going into Phase C

summary.title: Influence Track Priorities
bullets: Gray (council) = guards, Orange (merchant) = coins — priority early
Purple (cleric) = pilgrim steps — valuable with monastery synergies
Green (scholar) = VP — late-game powerhouse
Focus 1-2 tracks early, branch out later

summary.title: The Pilgrim Map
bullets: Monasteries give free influence advances, VP, diplomas, and abilities
Plan your route early — synergize with character card strategy
Some monasteries give 3 influence spaces free — enormous value
Purple dice and card bonuses provide movement

summary.title: Character Card Evaluation
bullets: Check: class, influence value, bonus type, cost type, diploma icons
Phase E bonuses are generally most valuable (trigger every round)
Diploma sets of 5 different icons = 12 VP — massive scoring
Match card class to influence tracks you are building

summary.title: Crown and Player Order Strategy
bullets: Crowns = crown tokens + favor tile crowns + player order flag crowns
First player drafts dice first and invests in voyages first
Castle is the main crown source — balance with city acquisitions
Especially important in Rounds 1 and 4

summary.title: Voyage Investment Timing
bullets: Voyage cards score 8-12+ VP based on end-game conditions
Invest early if affordable — no discount for waiting
Favor tile reducing cost by 2 is extremely powerful
Passing every round means missing major VP opportunities

walkthrough.title: The Guard Engine
walkthrough.text: The guard engine focuses on advancing the gray council member influence track and drafting gray dice for massive guard income. With your disk at position 10 or higher on the council member track, each gray die gives you 5 or more guards per round. Two gray dice at that level means 10+ guards per round — enough to buy expensive cards and invest in guard-cost voyages without worry. This strategy pairs well with council member cards that have Phase E bonuses granting extra guards or influence advances when you draft gray dice.

walkthrough.title: The Coin Engine
walkthrough.text: The coin engine mirrors the guard engine but focuses on the orange merchant influence track. High merchant track positions yield large coin income from orange dice. Coins buy character cards with coin costs and coin-cost voyages. This strategy excels when the character display is heavy on coin-cost cards, since other players competing for guard-cost cards will leave coin-cost cards available. Look for merchant Phase E bonus cards that give extra coins per orange die for compounding returns.

walkthrough.title: The Pilgrim Strategy
walkthrough.text: The pilgrim strategy focuses on the purple cleric track for pilgrim movement income, combined with voyage cards that reward monastery presence. Advance quickly on the cleric track — each purple die gives steps based on your position. Visit as many monasteries as possible, especially those with immediate bonuses that advance influence tracks or provide diplomas. Key voyage cards to target: Barbados (1 VP per monastery with your disk), Ceuta (1 VP per monastery without your disk — invest early before visiting), and Brasil (2 VP per I-level monastery visited). The monastery that gives 1 VP per future monastery visit is especially powerful when reached early.

walkthrough.title: The Diploma Collector
walkthrough.text: Diploma icons appear on character cards and monastery tiles. A complete set of 5 different diploma icons scores 12 victory points, and you can form multiple sets. Wild diplomas count as any icon. This strategy targets character cards with diploma icons, especially those with 2 icons, and monasteries that provide diploma bonuses. The monastery that lets you score diploma sets mid-game is a powerful accelerator. With 2 full sets and some singles, you can score 25+ points from diplomas alone. Prioritize wild diploma sources — they complete sets without needing specific icons.

walkthrough.title: The Victory Point Engine
walkthrough.text: The green scholar track gives direct victory point income in Phase E. At high positions, each green die can grant 5+ VP per round. This is pure points with no intermediate conversion needed — you do not need to buy cards or invest in voyages to convert the income. The challenge is that advancing on the scholar track does not directly help you acquire cards or fund voyages. Pair this with a guard or coin engine on the side. In Rounds 3 and 4, two green dice at position 13+ can give you 20+ VP from income alone.

walkthrough.title: Castle Control Strategy
walkthrough.text: Placing dice in the castle is free and gives you favor tiles with powerful bonuses. The cap tile favor adds 3 to a die's value, potentially turning a 4 into a 7 for placement and cost purposes — devastating for sniping priority in a city. The voyage cost reduction tile saves 2 on every voyage for the entire round. The 7 guards or 7 coins tile is a massive resource injection. And the 4 VP plus invest in a voyage tile gives points and a free voyage action. Experienced players contest the castle heavily in Round 1 to set the tone. Remember: castle dice use ascending order, so lower values go left and act first.

walkthrough.title: The White Die Advantage
walkthrough.text: The white die is the most flexible die in the game. In Phase E, it lets you choose any influence track's income — pick whichever track you are highest on for maximum reward. This means the white die's value increases as you advance on any single track. If you are at position 13 on the scholar track, a white die gives you 10 VP. Early in the game when all tracks are low, the white die is less impactful. But by Rounds 3 and 4, it can be the most valuable die in the pool. Prioritize it when you have one dominant influence track.

walkthrough.title: Phase E Bonus Card Synergies
walkthrough.text: Phase E bonus character cards are the most consistently powerful cards in the game because they trigger every round. Cards that give +1 influence on any track at the start of Phase E compound over 3-4 rounds, potentially advancing you 3-4 spaces for free. Cards that give extra resources per die of a certain color amplify your engine. Cards that let you exchange resources for VP at a 2-to-1 rate prevent waste when your income exceeds the 20 resource cap. Acquire Phase E bonus cards as early as possible — their value is proportional to the number of remaining rounds.

walkthrough.title: End-Game Scoring Optimization
walkthrough.text: In the final scoring, there are 5 categories. First, voyage tasks can score 6 to 16+ VP per card — invest in 2-3 voyages aligned with your strategy. Second, influence track majorities award the top player 7 to 14 VP per track, with second and third place earning less. You do not need to win every track — focus on 1-2 where you are dominant. Third, diploma sets scale exponentially — 12 for a set of 5 versus just 2 for a pair. Fourth, game end bonus cards reward specific conditions — check them early and build toward them. Fifth, remaining guards plus coins plus crowns divided by 2 is often 5-10 free points. Do not spend everything on your last voyage if holding resources scores more.

walkthrough.title: Adapting to the Character Display
walkthrough.text: Every game of Coimbra presents a different character display each round, and the best players adapt. In Phase B, scan all 12 character cards before drafting your first die. Identify which cards you want, what they cost, where they are, and what class they are. If the display is heavy on one class, the influence track competition for that class will be fierce. If guard-cost cards dominate, coins become less critical and vice versa. The voyage cards also shape your strategy — check them during setup and plan which ones to target. Flexibility and reading the board state are what separate good Coimbra players from great ones.

summary.title: The Coin Engine
bullets: Focus orange merchant track — orange dice yield large coin income

Less competition if other players focus on guards
Look for merchant Phase E bonus cards for compounding returns

summary.title: The Pilgrim Strategy
bullets: Advance purple cleric track — purple dice give pilgrim steps

Key voyages: Barbados (VP per monastery), Brasil (VP per I-monastery)
Monastery giving 1 VP per future visit is powerful early

Target cards with 2 diploma icons and wild diploma sources

bullets: Favor tiles are free and provide powerful bonuses

Contest castle heavily in Round 1 to set the tone

bullets: Scan all 12 cards before drafting your first die
Identify costs, classes, and bonus types across the display
If one class dominates, expect fierce competition on that track
Flexibility and reading the board state separate good from great

entries.definition: One of the 5 dice colors. In Phase E, a gray die provides guard income based on your position on the gray council member influence track. The higher your disk on the track, the more guards you receive.
One of the 5 dice colors. In Phase E, an orange die provides coin income based on your position on the orange merchant influence track.
One of the 5 dice colors. In Phase E, a purple die provides pilgrim movement steps based on your position on the purple cleric influence track.
One of the 5 dice colors. In Phase E, a green die provides victory points based on your position on the green scholar influence track.
One of the 5 dice colors. In Phase E, a white die lets you choose any one influence track and collect that track's income reward. Does not count as any specific color for Phase E bonus card effects.
One of the 4 character classes. Associated with the gray influence track. The council member track provides guard income in Phase E. There are 14 council member character cards in the game.
One of the 4 character classes. Associated with the orange influence track. The merchant track provides coin income in Phase E. There are 14 merchant character cards in the game.
One of the 4 character classes. Associated with the purple influence track. The cleric track provides pilgrim movement steps in Phase E. There are 14 cleric character cards in the game.
One of the 4 character classes. Associated with the green influence track. The scholar track provides victory point income in Phase E. There are 14 scholar character cards in the game.

There are 4 influence tracks on the game board, one for each character class: council member (gray), merchant (orange), cleric (purple), and scholar (green). Each track goes from 0 to a maximum of 15. Your position determines your Phase E income reward and contributes to end-game majority scoring.

The number printed on a character card that determines how many spaces you advance on the matching influence track when you acquire that card. Start character cards and regular character cards both have influence values.

One of 4 tiles placed above the influence tracks during setup. At game end, each tile awards victory points for first place, second place, and (in 4-player games) third place on the track below it. Tiles are double-sided for different player counts.

The topmost city location on the game board. Dice here follow ascending order (low to high, left to right). The castle action is free: take one available favor tile and gain its immediate bonus. No guard or coin cost is required.

The second city location from the top. Dice here follow descending order (high to low, left to right). The action is to acquire one character card from the row next to the upper city, paying guards or coins equal to your die's value.
The third city location from the top. Same descending dice order and character card acquisition rules as the upper city, but uses the central city's row of character cards.
The bottom city location. Same descending dice order and character card acquisition rules as the other cities, but uses the lower city's row of character cards.

Each player has 3 die holders placed on the shutter spaces of their player board. When drafting a die, place it in a holder first, then onto a city location. After Phase C, the die returns to its holder on a shutter space for Phase E.

The 3 spaces on your player board where die holders sit. After retrieving dice in Phase C, place each die (in its holder) on an empty shutter space. The dice remain there through Phase E for income collection.

Each player's personal board showing the guard track (0-20), coin track (0-20), 3 shutter spaces for die holders, and zones for character cards (immediate bonus zone, Phase C bonus zone, Phase E bonus zone, game end bonus zone).
A track on the player board running from 0 to 20. Guards are spent to acquire character cards with guard cost icons and to invest in guard-cost voyages. You start the game at 7 guards.
A track on the player board running from 0 to 20. Coins are spent to acquire character cards with coin cost icons and to invest in coin-cost voyages. You start the game at 7 coins.

One of the two main resources in Coimbra, tracked on the guard track of your player board. Used to pay for guard-cost character cards and guard-cost voyage investments. Maximum of 20. Leftover guards at game end contribute to final scoring.

One of the two main resources in Coimbra, tracked on the coin track of your player board. Used to pay for coin-cost character cards and coin-cost voyage investments. Maximum of 20. Leftover coins at game end contribute to final scoring.

Used to determine player order in Phase D. Sources include crown tokens from character cards, crowns on favor tiles, and crowns next to your current player order flag. More crowns means earlier player order next round. Crowns also contribute to final scoring as part of the guards+coins+crowns total.

One of 4 tokens (one per character class) placed on the character display during setup and at the end of each round. When you acquire a card with a crown token, you take the token for Phase D crown counting.

One of 4 tiles displayed beside the castle each round. Taking a favor tile in the castle during Phase C is free. Each tile provides an immediate bonus and contributes 0-3 crowns to Phase D player order determination.

A special tile placed above the 2-crown favor tile. When a player takes the favor tile with the cap tile, they may place the cap tile on one of their dice in a city location, increasing that die's value by 3 (up to a combined maximum of 9). The increased value affects both placement position and cost.

One of 15 cards, 6 of which are displayed at the bottom of the game board each game. Players invest in voyages during Phase F by paying the card's cost. At game end, each invested voyage scores VP based on its specific task (monasteries visited, diplomas collected, influence positions, etc.).

One of 56 cards in the game (8 start cards with I-backs, 24 with II-backs, 24 with III-backs). Each has a class, influence value, and a bonus (immediate, Phase C, Phase E, or game end). Acquired during Phase C by paying guards or coins equal to your die value.

One of 8 cards with I-backs drafted during setup. Each provides an influence value for its class track and either an immediate bonus or a Phase C bonus. Players draft 2 start cards each before the game begins.

A one-time bonus triggered when a character card is acquired or when a monastery is visited. Depicted with a lightning bolt symbol. Cards with immediate bonuses are placed in the immediate bonus zone to the right of your player board.

A bonus found only on start character cards that triggers once each time you acquire a specific type of character card during Phase C. Cards with Phase C bonuses are placed below your player board in the Phase C bonus zone.

A recurring bonus that triggers during Phase E of each round. These are among the most powerful bonuses because they compound over multiple rounds. Cards with Phase E bonuses are placed below your player board in the Phase E bonus zone.

A bonus scored once during final scoring based on a specific condition (e.g., position on an influence track, number of cards of a class, etc.). Cards with game end bonuses are placed at the bottom right of your player board in the game end bonus zone.

Taken in Phase C if you cannot or do not want to perform a city location's normal action. Gain up to 2 coins AND up to 2 guards. This replaces the normal action of acquiring a character card or taking a favor tile.

The Phase F action. Choose a voyage card without your disk, pay its cost in guards or coins as indicated, and place a disk on its rose. You may invest in one voyage per round. Each voyage can hold multiple players' disks.

Your wooden figure that moves along the roads of the pilgrim map. Movement is gained from purple die income, character card bonuses, and monastery bonuses. Your first step must go along the one-way road from your Coimbra starting corner.

The network of roads, stopovers, and monasteries on the right side of the game board. Contains 8 blue (I) monastery spaces, 4 brown (II) monastery spaces, and 2 ruby (III) monastery spaces, plus the central starting corners of Coimbra.

A location on the pilgrim map. When your pilgrim moves onto a monastery, you must place one of your disks above it (if you do not already have one there). Placing a disk gives you the monastery's bonus. There are 14 monastery spaces total: 8 blue (I), 4 brown (II), and 2 ruby (III).
A location on the pilgrim map between monasteries. Nothing happens when you stop on or pass through a stopover. They exist only as pathway connections between monasteries.
One of 8 monastery spaces on the pilgrim map closest to Coimbra's center. Filled with tiles from the I-stack during setup. These are the first monasteries reachable by your pilgrim.
One of 4 monastery spaces farther out on the pilgrim map. Filled with tiles from the II-stack during setup. Generally offers stronger bonuses than blue monasteries.
One of 2 monastery spaces at the far edges of the pilgrim map. Filled with tiles from the III-stack during setup. Offers the most powerful bonuses but requires significant pilgrim movement to reach.

A symbol found on some character cards and monastery tiles. There are 5 regular diploma types and 1 wild diploma. At game end, sets of different diploma icons score VP: 5=12, 4=8, 3=4, 2=2, single=1. Each diploma can only be used in one set.
A special diploma icon that can substitute for any of the 5 regular diploma types when forming sets during final scoring. Found on certain character cards and monastery tiles.

At game end, form sets from your diploma icons. A complete set of 5 different icons scores 12 VP. 4 different = 8 VP. 3 different = 4 VP. 2 different = 2 VP. A single diploma = 1 VP. Wild diplomas can fill any slot. No diploma may be used in more than one set.

At game end, for each of the 4 influence tracks, the player farthest up scores first place VP from the influence scoring tile above that track. Second place and (in 4-player games) third place also score. Disks on space 0 earn nothing. In 2-player games, second place only scores if within 3 spaces of first.
At game end, sum your remaining guards, coins, and crowns, then divide by 2 (rounded down) to get VP. This rewards efficient resource management and avoiding overspending on your last voyage.
The track around the edge of the game board where players track their victory points throughout the game. Each player places a disk on space 0 during setup.
Numbered flags (1 through 4) on the game board where players place their lions to indicate turn order. Each flag also shows crowns that count toward Phase D player order determination.
Each player's wooden lion piece placed on a player order flag to indicate their position in turn order. Lions are moved during Phase D when new player order is determined by crown totals.

Each player has 25 wooden disks. Used to mark positions on influence tracks, the score track, monastery visits, and voyage investments. 5 are placed during setup; the remaining 20 form your personal supply.

Neutral tokens used in 2- and 3-player games placed in city locations during setup. When triggered in Phase C, they remove the highest-influence-value card from that city's row to the discard pile, simulating a phantom player.

The combined deck of II-back and III-back character cards (II on top of III). 12 cards are drawn from this stack each round to form the character display. The game ends after Round 4 when this stack is empty.
The 3x4 grid of 12 face-up character cards arranged in 3 rows next to the upper, central, and lower cities. Refreshed at the end of each round. Cards are acquired during Phase C.
A face-up pile next to the character stack where unacquired character cards are placed at the end of Phase C evaluations. Some character card bonuses let you choose a card from the discard pile.
The first phase of each round. The first player rolls all dice from the pool to create the dice pool for the round. Die values range from 1 to 6.

entries.term: Phase B: Drafting and Placing Dice
entries.definition: The second phase. In player order, players take turns drafting one die at a time from the pool and placing it in a city location. Each player places exactly 3 dice per round.

The third phase. City locations are evaluated top to bottom (castle, upper, central, lower). Within each location, dice trigger left to right. Players perform the location's action (take favor tile or acquire card) and retrieve their die.
The fourth phase. Players count their total crowns and rearrange lions on the player order flags. Most crowns = flag 1. Ties reverse relative order.
The fifth phase. In new player order, each player activates their 3 dice by color to collect income rewards from matching influence tracks. Dice are returned to the pool afterward.

entries.term: Phase F: Invest in a Voyage
entries.definition: The sixth and final phase. In player order, each player may optionally invest in one voyage card by paying its cost and placing a disk on it.
Voyage card. For each voyage card with your disk on it (including this one), gain 2 VP. Rewards investing in multiple voyages.
Voyage card. For each diploma you have (irrespective of kind), gain 1 VP. Rewards the diploma collector strategy.
Voyage card. Check two depicted influence tracks, sum both values, divide by 2 (rounded down), gain the result as VP.
Voyage card. Check two depicted influence tracks, sum both values, divide by 2 (rounded down), gain the result as VP. Different track pairing than Cabo Verde.
Voyage card. For each monastery that does NOT have your disk above it, gain 1 VP. Best invested in early before visiting many monasteries.
Voyage card. Sum your guards, coins, and crowns, divide by 2 (rounded down), gain result as VP. Rewards resource hoarding.
Voyage card. Double the value of your least developed influence track and gain that as VP. Rewards balanced influence track development.
Voyage card. For each monastery with your disk above it, gain 1 VP. Pairs perfectly with the pilgrim strategy.
Voyage card. For each Level I monastery with your disk above it, gain 2 VP. Rewards visiting the blue monasteries closest to the starting corners.
Voyage card. For each character card beyond your 10th, gain 3 VP. Rewards aggressive card acquisition over all 4 rounds.
Voyage card. Gain a flat 9 VP regardless of game state. The simplest voyage — guaranteed points for the investment cost.
Voyage card. For each of your character cards of the depicted class, gain 2 VP. Rewards concentrating on one character class.
Voyage card. For each of your character cards of the depicted class, gain 2 VP. Similar to Macau but for a different class.
The area to the right of your player board where character cards with immediate bonuses are placed after their one-time bonus is resolved.
The area below your player board where start character cards with Phase C bonuses are placed. These bonuses trigger once each time you acquire a matching character card type during Phase C.
The area below your player board where character cards with Phase E bonuses are placed. These trigger every round during Phase E, making them compound over time.
The area at the bottom right of your player board where character cards with game end bonuses are placed. These score once during final scoring based on specific conditions.
